2604.19125.title: Do Emotions Influence Moral Judgment in Large Language Models?
2604.19125.paper_summary: Large language models have been extensively studied for emotion recognition and moral reasoning as distinct capabilities, yet the extent to which emotions influence moral judgment remains underexplored. In this work, we develop an emotion-induction pipeline that infuses emotion into moral situations and evaluate shifts in moral acceptability across multiple datasets and LLMs. We observe a directional pattern: positive emotions increase moral acceptability and negative emotions decrease it, with effects strong enough to reverse binary moral judgments in up to 20% of cases, and with susceptibility scaling inversely with model capability. Our analysis further reveals that specific emotions can sometimes behave contrary to what their valence would predict (e.g., remorse paradoxically increases acceptability). A complementary human annotation study shows humans do not exhibit these systematic shifts, indicating an alignment gap in current LLMs.
2604.19125.paper_summary_zh: 大型語言模型在情感識別和道德推理作為不同能力方面已被廣泛研究，但情感對道德判斷的影響程度仍然未被充分探討。<lb/>在這項工作中，我們開發了一個情感誘導管道，將情感注入道德情境中，並評估多個數據集和大型語言模型中道德可接受性的變化。<lb/>我們觀察到一種方向性模式：正向情感增加道德可接受性，而負向情感則減少，這些影響強大到足以在多達20%的案例中逆轉二元道德判斷，且易感性與模型能力呈反比。<lb/>我們的分析進一步揭示，特定情感有時會表現出與其價值預測相反的行為（例如，懊悔反而增加可接受性）。<lb/>一項補充的人類標註研究顯示，人類並不表現出這些系統性的變化，這表明當前大型語言模型存在對齊差距。
2604.19125.author: Mohammad Saim et.al.
